critique.summary: The article presents a directory of over 135 open hardware devices that users can flash with their own firmware. It highlights a variety of development boards, modules, and single-board computers suitable for applications ranging from IoT and AI to industrial automation and smart home projects.
mainPoints: The directory features a wide range of open hardware devices including development boards, sensor modules, and single-board computers.
Devices support flashing custom firmware, enabling users to customize hardware for specific applications.
Products listed cover diverse use cases such as AI cameras, wireless sensor networks, industrial automation, smart home control, and edge AI.
Prices and technical specifications are provided for each device, facilitating informed purchasing decisions.
The directory emphasizes user control and flexibility in hardware and firmware choices.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes a curated list of open hardware devices available for purchase, including technical specifications and typical use cases. The information aligns with publicly available product details from manufacturers and distributors. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, as it primarily serves as a product directory rather than investigative journalism or analytical reporting.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential challenges or limitations of using open hardware, such as compatibility issues or required technical expertise.
There is no mention of the security implications of flashing custom firmware on these devices.
The environmental impact or sustainability of the hardware products is not addressed.
Information about community support, software ecosystem maturity, or documentation quality for these devices is missing.
The article does not provide comparative analysis or user reviews to help evaluate device performance or reliability.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion critiques the open hardware directory, noting its limited coverage compared to established repositories and questioning the accuracy of its open hardware claims. Some users appreciate the discovery aspect, while others highlight usability and categorization issues.

comments.author: haddonist
comments.text:
Looks good, but it'll probably take a while until it's anywhere close to the coverage of existing repositories:

https://templates.blakadder.com/ has almost 3,000 devices flashable onto Tasmota firmware.

For older Tuya devices there's https://github.com/tuya-cloudcutter/tuya-cloudcutter

OpenBeken https://github.com/openshwprojects/OpenBK7231T_App covers 800 of the newer generation Tuya devices.

And there's a large community adapting ESP32 devices onto https://esphome.io/
comments.analysis: This comment provides specific examples of existing repositories with broader device coverage, offering a well-informed and constructive comparison.

comments.author: Karliss
comments.text:
The link was posted by project's author so probably should have been Show HN.

Feels more like AI slop list of "a bunch of hardware that you can buy from hobbyist electronic stores" which has no idea what it wants to be, shiny on surface but deeper you look less sense it makes. Not a surprised, the company who made it (likely single person) describes itself as "We're crafting interesting tools to speed up software development using Artificial Intelligence."

Good chunk of that stuff is not open hardware by any definition -> neither the hardware design being open nor the firmware not even community written firmware for proprietary hardware.

If you ignore the poor description of the site is the parametric search at least good? The values in parameter dropdowns seem to be filled based on currently displayed items, that might be fine for narrowing down once you already made a search but for initial search it means you get random subset of available values. The fact that whole thing is non-categorized, random mix of mismatched type of hardware doesn't make the parametric search better. Good parametric search needs well curated and structured database of descriptions made by people who understand corresponding product category, otherwise it's garbage in garbage out.

Having to wait half a minute while AI is reticulating splines even when you used quite specific keywords isn't a good search experience either.

So if it's not a good list of open hardware, not a good list of hardware you can flash open firmware, not a good search for electronic components what is it good for? Only value I see is as a fuzzy set of links and tags for exploring a subset of related hardware topics.
comments.analysis: The comment critically evaluates the site's search functionality and categorization, highlighting valid usability concerns and the questionable openness of many listed devices.

comments.author: anonymous_user9
comments.text:
AI slop. Most of the things on this list are not open hardware, and some of the items are completely proprietary. For example, the SLAMTEC RPLiDAR A1 [1]. This part doesn't even have user-upgradeable firmware.

[1] https://openhardware.directory/devices/slamtec-rplidar-a1
comments.analysis: This comment points out specific examples of non-open hardware on the list, reinforcing concerns about the directory's accuracy in representing open hardware.

comments.author: zdw
comments.text: There are another ~3k devices on the OpenWRT table of hardware that would fall into this category: https://toh.openwrt.org/
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant alternative resource with a large device list, contributing useful context to the discussion.

comments.author: skyberrys
comments.text:
Well I fell down an RGB keyboard rabbit hole there. At first I thought I wouldn't find something compelling in this list but it only took me to the second scroll to get drawn in. The end result was a customizable keyboard from Canada.

Can I give it a link to my weird (but open) hardware?
comments.analysis: Expresses a positive user experience and interest in contributing, adding a personal and constructive perspective.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.analysis: Briefly notes pricing concerns, which may be relevant but lacks detail or supporting evidence.

comments.author: Neywiny
comments.text: Feels weird to advertise a microcontroller dev board this way. But the other stuff is cool
comments.analysis: Provides a subjective opinion on marketing style but offers limited substantive critique.

comments.author: randyrand
comments.text: more often than not, you could just buy the same new CPU on the open market and swap it in. It bypasses secure boot, etc.
comments.analysis: Makes a technical point about hardware modification, but the comment is vague and lacks context.
